meta.subtitle: My first impression of [Las Vegas] when I just moved here was...
well, the casinos.
As soon as I got off the plane, I saw so many casinos and their lights.
There were so many colors, it was really dazzling.
Especially when you just arrive in Vegas, [you feel Las Vegas is]
many casinos, many buildings. In recent years, even though
some casinos have closed down, there are always new casinos emerging,
so on that Strip, you always feel it's still Vegas.
I felt like this place is a gambling city...
Casinos both big and small lined the streets of Las Vegas,
and I found it very majestic.
What others see is just the Las Vegas Strip and think it's bustling,
but we really live in Las Vegas,
and you can't go to the casino every day or visit the Strip every day.
Just for me, so far, I still live here for my job and my life,
and I feel comfortable.
I feel that life here [in Las Vegas] is not lonely at all.
It is very enriching.
Our family in Vietnam was not very well off,
and the political situation in the country was not very stable,
so we decided to move to Las Vegas.
I used to live in Reno, which is a smaller city in northern Nevada,
but the business at its casinos is not good now,
[so the abundance of jobs offered by the Strip is] why I moved here.
I have lived in Las Vegas for over thirty years now.
My impression is pretty great--the location is good,
everyone's life is fairly stable,
my family members can all find jobs.
We all have work to do.
I think in regards to life here, actually, you shouldn't talk about the Strip.
Without the Strip, it's a good city for your retirement.
When I retire, I still miss Las Vegas. Even if I move to China, maybe at least about
three or four months [of the year], I will come back to Vegas
I feel so convenient [here]. Not a problem. I like specials, you know, 24 hour
[operation hours at] the bar. Sometimes when I get off I stop by there just relax,
spend $10 or $20 for the quarter [slot] machine and
get a couple of drink before I got home, you know.
I really enjoy singing and dancing, and in Las Vegas, if you didn't know,
there are so many people who like singing and dancing--
of course, I'm quite busy so I can't go every day.
Moreover, the level of art in Las Vegas is very high,
so if I want to dance, every day I can dance.
A deeper impression of Vegas is that....
it is hot, very hot in Vegas.
Because I have lived in New York and lived here,
[I know] the weather is actually very different.
Even my children don't want to come here [because of the weather].
[laughs]
They are in New York, they are not here.
Shanghai [my hometown] has always been very humid,
and the summer is extremely hot,
but the heat in summer there versus the heat from here... it is completely different.
This--It is very dry here, it is not humidity.
At the time [I first moved here], the city had few houses
few people,
of the streets...
there were only three, five, six, or seven streets.
This is because many areas were undeveloped,
there were just not a lot of people here.
Living here [presently] is nice, [the city] has built many new houses,
the new areas are good too,
and the house prices are reasonable,
cheaper compared to Reno,
much cheaper compared to other states, especially California,
so many people have moved here from other states.
Las Vegas didn't have this many people before; now there are quite a few.
When I first moved here, there were only a few hundred thousand people,
and now there are more than a million.
Chinatown, um, how to say...
is very similar to Taiwan or China's Tempo-style buildings. Moreover,
The lifestyle is also quite similar; for example, on Spring Mountain Rd
you can find many Chinese people living a lifestyle akin to one in China.
It has many shops, places to eat,
and numerous markets.
When you're there, you can, um--
When you first arrive in the U.S., you can easily live on this road,
and you won't feel like you have come to America.
You will feel like, 'oh this is very similar to being in a certain city in China.'
Ah, what gives me the most sense of belonging... What gives me a lot of happiness is
that Las Vegas has many Chinese people, especially retirees [like me].
Coming here, the sense of belonging, I think, is great,
because many Chinese people are very helpful.
Um, and very well-received, I think, very well-received because
when I first arrived in the 1990s, there weren't many Chinese people and
not many new immigrants
so whenever new immigrants came and needed-
needed help...
many people were willing to help. So I felt very happy,
and welcomed,
and I had a strong sense of belonging.
In the United States, it is actually very difficult to define a sense of belonging
because a true sense of belonging, if you feel it, is actually in places like Chinatown.
It is a relatively different place. [When you are in Chinatown] aside from
taking the bus, or sometimes seeing foreigners, everything else is also
the voices of Chinese people,
and the people you see are also Chinese.
When I first moved here, um, I felt the most sense of belonging in, um...
the library.
Because when I moved here, I was still young,
and I only had reading to rely on. Every time I went to the library,
it was very large, with many books inside,
and I saw different races, and they were all very friendly.
If you don't understand something, [people at the library] will teach you.
For example, in English, people will engage in conversation.
They will guide you on how to use English, so I thought it was very pleasant.
They are very enthusiastic about helping new immigrants.
Regarding the sense of belonging, I feel that
what is really good and what really helps me is
that there are many Chinese supermarkets here. Because for us [Chinese people],
regarding your lifestyle, what you eat every day is the most important.
[When I moved here] there weren't many [Chinese] markets here, and they weren't complete.
So many times I would drive
to California, to Los Angeles,
just to buy food.
Now that Chinatown is here, many things are complete,
so there is no need to go to other cities to buy things.
